description:
Goal: give compile-time macro execution a real compiler-host surface that records compiler service requests as source-production commands and applies them through the .11.7.4 publication boundary with the live scope/session, without giving runtime/native code compiler authority.

Signal:
- macro code can call Fz.Compiler compiler-host externs with AST roots from the active quoted source heap; the interpreter records host commands with raw roots, source identity, and caller scope
- source production drains those commands through source_publish / ScopeSession, producing the same FunctionSource, module, type, protocol, and impl facts as literal source
- normal backend/native execution has no compiler-host context; attempts to call compiler-host externs fail without authority instead of silently mutating compiler state
- no World mutation occurs through unsafe interpreter aliases while Types is borrowed; host commands are applied at the source-production boundary
- telemetry records host calls as raw compiler ids and quoted-source keys so tests can compare command publication to literal-source publication

Change:
- add a compile-time host context / command buffer to backend interpreter runs used by macro execution
- define the Fz.Compiler surface and extern dispatch only for compile-time macro runs
- apply recorded compiler commands through the quoted-source publication boundary and ScopeSession, not directly from the interpreter
- add tests proving compiler-service definitions publish the same facts as literal source definitions and proving runtime roots cannot access compiler authority

Why:
- Fz.Compiler needs source-session authority; doing it inside macro runtime readiness would either fake the authority or alias World unsafely while interpreting
- compiler-service calls are source production, not macro execution mechanics
- recursive macro expansion should consume this command stream rather than grow a second publication path
title: Compiler2 macro host services: Fz.Compiler command publication
